beats.narration: A radiograph can be ruined before the first photon. Unprocessed film remembers every insult and prints it, often disguised as a defect.
Handle it like evidence: clean, dry hands; edges and corners only; and move slowly, since fast pulls spark static that prints black tree-shaped marks.
Bend a sheet and you crimp it: a crescent mark. Kinked before exposure it images light; after exposure, dark. Crimps mimic real flaws.

payload.heading: Crimp marks tell time
payload.note: LIGHT = BENT BEFORE · DARK = BENT AFTER

beats.narration: In storage, flat stacks press marks, so store boxes on edge; keep stock cool, dry, and shielded from radiation; rotate first in, first out.
Here's the rule: unprocessed film remembers everything. Handle it gently, store it safely, and your radiograph will have nothing to confess later.

payload.sub: Protect it now — nothing to confess later.
